channel.title: Nature Methods
channel.description: Nature Methods offers a unique interdisciplinary forum for the publication of novel methods. Nature Methods focuses on the life sciences, combining practical, technique-driven subject matter with rigorous peer-review standards to ensure that readers are consistently presented with only the most valuable and highest quality methodological research. The journal offers its readers primary research papers as well as an array of opinions, reviews and short journalistic pieces to provide busy researchers with a broad, yet easily absorbed perspective of important methodological developments in the life sciences.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: TADShop: systematic benchmarking and identification of topologically associating domains
item.content:encoded:
Nature Methods, Published online: 27 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41592-026-03100-2

This study benchmarks 43 computational methods for identifying topologically associating domains (TADs), presents a tool based on a consensus strategy that leads to improved performance, and releases a web service to benchmark and use TAD calling methods.
item.dc:title: TADShop: systematic benchmarking and identification of topologically associating domains
item.dc:creator: Pumin Li
Andras Hatos
Miljan Petrovic
Gian Marco Franceschini
Luca Nanni
Daniele Tavernari
Giovanni Ciriello

item.title: External validation improves generalizability, replicability and reproducibility in predictive models for neuroimaging
item.content:encoded:
Nature Methods, Published online: 27 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41592-026-03115-9

This Perspective promotes the use of external validation using independent datasets for human neuroimaging studies.
item.dc:title: External validation improves generalizability, replicability and reproducibility in predictive models for neuroimaging
item.dc:creator: Matthew Rosenblatt
Maya L. Foster
Brendan D. Adkinson
Link Tejavibulya
Milana Khaitova
Jean Ye
Huili Sun
Raimundo X. Rodriguez
Chris C. Camp
Ash Chinta
Marie C. McCusker
Ling Han
Christopher T. Fields
Saloni Mehta
Dustin Scheinost

item.title: Adding chemical identity to cryo-electron microscopy
item.content:encoded:
Nature Methods, Published online: 25 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41592-026-03112-y

A correlative workflow combining cryo-electron microscopy and cryo-electron tomography with secondary ion mass spectrometry generates spatially registered chemical maps from the same vitrified sample, directly linking ultrastructural information with molecular composition. This advance opens new avenues for identifying where drugs, pollutants and signaling molecules reside within cells at nanoscale resolution.
item.dc:title: Adding chemical identity to cryo-electron microscopy
item.dc:creator: Julia Peukes

item.title: LGBTQ+ allies to count on
item.content:encoded:
Nature Methods, Published online: 25 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41592-026-03101-1

Support for colleagues can take many forms. However and whenever it arrives, it is welcome.
item.dc:title: LGBTQ+ allies to count on
item.dc:creator: Vivien Marx

item.title: Teaching an old dog new cells
item.content:encoded:
Nature Methods, Published online: 25 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41592-026-03113-x

Two new methods show that bulk-trained sequence models can be efficiently adapted to predict gene regulation and variant effects at single-cell resolution.
item.dc:title: Teaching an old dog new cells
item.dc:creator: Johannes Linder
David R. Kelley

item.title: Subcellular chemical mapping using correlated cryogenic electron and mass spectrometry imaging
item.content:encoded:
Nature Methods, Published online: 25 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41592-026-03109-7

A correlative workflow combining cryo-EM and focused ion beam secondary ion mass spectrometry (FIB-SIMS) facilitates subcellular chemical analysis with high spatial resolution.
item.dc:title: Subcellular chemical mapping using correlated cryogenic electron and mass spectrometry imaging
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ochner
Buse Isbilir
Sonja Blasche
David Scheidweiler
Yuexuan Zhang
Zhexin Wang
Tom Smith
Catarina Franco
Rob Bradley
Kiran R. Patil
Tanmay A. M. Bharat

item.title: Multiplexed perturbation enables scalable pooled screens
item.content:encoded:
Nature Methods, Published online: 25 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41592-026-03095-w

This paper shows that high MOI sgRNA multiplexing maintains CRISPRi screen performance while enabling reduced cell numbers, challenging conventional reliance on low-MOI infections.
item.dc:title: Multiplexed perturbation enables scalable pooled screens
item.dc:creator: Stefan Oberlin
Neil Q. Tay
Albert Xue
Ruzbeh Mosadeghi
Harold Pimentel
Michael T. McManus

item.title: Decoding sequence determinants of gene expression in diverse cellular and disease states
item.content:encoded:
Nature Methods, Published online: 25 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41592-026-03102-0

Decima learns to predict cell-type-specific gene expression based on DNA sequences, contributing to the analysis of gene regulation and design of context-specific regulatory elements.
item.dc:title: Decoding sequence determinants of gene expression in diverse cellular and disease states
item.dc:creator: Avantika Lal
Alexander Karollus
Laura Gunsalus
David Garfield
Surag Nair
Alex M. Tseng
M. Grace Gordon
John Blischak
Bryce Van De Geijn
Tushar Bhangale
Jenna L. Collier
Nathaniel Diamant
Tommaso Biancalani
Hector Corrada Bravo
Gabriele Scalia
Gokcen Eraslan
